subskills.name: Address-of operator (&) and why scanf needs it
Declaring typed pointers and common declaration pitfalls
Assigning addresses to pointers and dereferencing to read/write

questions.prompt: In C, what does `int *p, q;` declare?
choices: Both p and q are pointers to int
p is a pointer to int; q is an int
p is an int; q is a pointer to int
Both p and q are ints
questions.correct_answer: p is a pointer to int; q is an int
rubric: Understands that * binds to the declarator and does not distribute across comma-separated variables

student_answers.answer: Both p and q are pointers to int

evidence_chunks.text: More about declaring pointers The * operator does not distribute • Example • int *p, q; p is declared to be a pointer to int. • q is declared to be an int. • 9

results.notes: Student assumes * applies to both variables; it only applies to p.

learning_plan.explain: Explain that `*` attaches to each variable name. `int *p, q;` makes only p a pointer. To make both pointers use `int *p, *q;`.

micro_quiz.prompt: Which declaration makes both p and q pointers to int?

stop_condition.reason: Single misconception found; micro-quiz checks the exact fix.

subskills.name: Address-of operator (&) and why scanf needs it
Declaring typed pointers and common declaration pitfalls
Assigning addresses to pointers and dereferencing to read/write

questions.prompt: Why do we write `scanf("%d", &x);` instead of `scanf("%d", x);`?
questions.correct_answer: scanf needs the address of x so it can store the input value into x
rubric: Mentions scanf writes into memory at a provided address

student_answers.answer: Because & converts the input into an integer

evidence_chunks.text: Address Operator A variable can be referenced using the address • operator & Example: scanf(“%f”, &x); This statement specifies that the value read is to be • stored at the address of x 4

results.notes: Student does not know & means address-of or that scanf needs a write location.

learning_plan.explain: Define `&x` as the memory address of x. Explain scanf needs a pointer so it can store the value at that address.

stop_condition.reason: Core definition gap; quick check validates understanding.

subskills.name: Address-of operator (&) and why scanf needs it
Declaring typed pointers and common declaration pitfalls
Assigning addresses to pointers and dereferencing to read/write

questions.prompt: Assume an int is 4 bytes. If `int *p` currently holds address 1000, what address will p hold after `p++;`?

evidence_chunks.text: Pointer Arithmetic The following arithmetic operations are supported • • +, -, ++, --, +=, -= • These operations determine pointer movements • Only integers may be used in these operations 15

Pointer Arithmetic Arithmetic is performed relative to the variable type being pointed to • Example: p++; • • when applied to pointers, ++ means increment pointer to point to next value in memory if p is defined as int *p, p will be incremented by 4 bytes (system dependent)

results.notes: Student treated pointer increment as byte-wise instead of type-scaled.

learning_plan.explain: Explain pointer arithmetic is scaled by the pointed-to type. For int*, p++ adds sizeof(int) bytes (commonly 4).

micro_quiz.prompt: If `double *d` holds address 2000 and doubles are 8 bytes, what address after `d++`?

stop_condition.reason: Single reasoning error; remediation plus micro-quiz targets scaling.
